title: Common String Methods
description: The String toolbox: length(), charAt(), substring(), indexOf(), contains(), split(), and more — every one of them returning a new String, per the Immutability lesson.
objectives: Use the most common String instance methods
Combine multiple String methods to solve a small text problem

sections.text: In 1D Arrays, array.length was a field (no parentheses). String is different: its size comes from a METHOD, length() — note the parentheses. This is one of the most common early mix-ups.
text.length (no parentheses) does not compile for a String — that syntax only works for arrays. String requires text.length() with parentheses, since it's a method call, not a field access.
Like arrays, charAt uses a 0-based index — text.charAt(0) is the first character, and the last valid index is text.length() - 1.
3. substring() — Extracting Part of a String

substring(start, end) includes the character at start but EXCLUDES the character at end — this is called "start-inclusive, end-exclusive." substring(0, 5) grabs indices 0,1,2,3,4 (5 characters: "Hello"), not index 5. substring(6) (one argument) simply goes from index 6 to the end of the String.
4. indexOf() and contains() — Searching Inside a String

sections.code:
String text = "Hello World";
System.out.println(text.indexOf("World"));  // 6
System.out.println(text.indexOf("xyz"));    // -1 (not found)
System.out.println(text.contains("World")); // true

sections.text: indexOf returns the starting index of the first match, or -1 if the text isn't found at all. contains simply returns true or false — use it when you only care WHETHER something appears, not WHERE.

sections.code:
String text = "  Hello World  ";
System.out.println(text.toUpperCase()); //   HELLO WORLD  
System.out.println(text.toLowerCase()); //   hello world  
System.out.println(text.trim());        // "Hello World" (no leading/trailing spaces)

sections.text: Per the Immutability lesson, none of these change text itself — each returns a brand new String that must be captured (or printed directly, as shown) to be used.
7. split() — Breaking a String Into Pieces
split() returns a String[] — an array, from the 1D Arrays lesson — so the enhanced for loop from that same module works here directly.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: .toLowerCase() on that result lowercases everything, giving cleaned
indexOf("@") finds the @ symbol's position in cleaned
substring(0, atIndex) grabs everything before the @ — the username

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing text.length instead of text.length() for a String — String's size is a method call, unlike an array's .length field.
Miscounting substring(start, end) bounds — remember end is EXCLUSIVE, so substring(0, 5) gives 5 characters (indices 0-4), not 6.
Using == instead of .equals() (revisited from the Immutability lesson) when checking String content.
Forgetting every String method call returns a new String, and not capturing the result — text.trim(); alone, with no assignment, has no visible effect.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does substring(start, end) include and exclude?
questions.answer: It includes the character at index start and excludes the character at index end — "start-inclusive, end-exclusive" — so it returns end - start characters total.

questions.question: What's the difference between indexOf() and contains() on a String?
questions.answer: indexOf() returns the starting index of the first match, or -1 if not found. contains() simply returns true or false, useful when you only need to know whether a substring is present, not where.

questions.question: Why is text.length correct for an array but not for a String?
questions.answer: An array's size is exposed as a field (.length, no parentheses), while String's size is exposed as a method (.length(), with parentheses) — they look similar but are different kinds of members.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: String provides a set of instance methods for common operations: length() for size, charAt(index) for a single character, substring(start, end) for extracting a range (start-inclusive, end-exclusive), indexOf()/contains() for searching, toUpperCase()/toLowerCase()/trim() for reformatting, equals()/equalsIgnoreCase() for comparison, and split() for breaking a String into an array of pieces. Since String is immutable, every one of these methods returns a brand new String (or array) rather than modifying the original — the result must be captured to actually use it.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: substring(start, end): start says HELLO, end says GOODBYE before it's included. length() needs parentheses (a String action), unlike an array's plain .length (a stored fact).

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What's the difference between indexOf and contains?
Why doesn't text.length work for a String the way it does for an array?
Does trim() modify the original String?

sections.title: Reverse String
sections.problemStatement: Write a function that reverses a string. The input string is given as an array of characters s, and must be modified in place with O(1) extra memory.
constraints: s[i] is a printable ASCII character
hints: Two pointers (Two Pointers lesson) from both ends, swapping and converging inward -- no extra array needed.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a reversed copy in a new array, then copy it back.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers swapping characters from both ends inward, in place.

sections.title: Valid Palindrome
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome after converting all uppercase letters to lowercase and removing all non-alphanumeric characters. (Same problem as covered under Two Pointers -- reframed here to highlight the String-cleaning angle specifically.)

examples.input: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"

hints: String methods like toLowerCase() and Character.isLetterOrDigit() do the cleaning; two pointers then check the palindrome property without building extra Strings.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a cleaned String using String methods, then compare it against its own reverse.
approaches.output: For s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama": true
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the cleaned String copies

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers directly on the original String, skipping non-alphanumeric characters, no extra String built.
approaches.output: For s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama": true

sections.title: Longest Common Prefix
sections.problemStatement: Write a function to find the longest common prefix string amongst an array of strings. If there is no common prefix, return an empty string.
hints: Use the first string as a candidate prefix, and shrink it (using substring) every time it fails to match the start of another string.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Compare characters at each position across ALL strings simultaneously, stopping at the first mismatch or shortest string.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m), n = number of strings, m = shortest string length

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Start with the first string as the prefix, and shrink it using substring/startsWith whenever it doesn't match the next string.

sections.title: Valid Anagram
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s (uses exactly the same characters, same counts, any order).
constraints: s and t consist of lowercase English letters
hints: Two Strings are anagrams if, once sorted, they're identical -- or, more efficiently, if their character counts match exactly (recall Hashing's frequency-counting pattern).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort both strings' characters and compare the results directly.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Count character frequencies with a fixed-size array -- increment for s, decrement for t; all counts must return to zero.

sections.title: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings needle and haystack, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if needle is not part of haystack.

examples.explanation: "sad" occurs at index 0 and index 6, so 0 (the first) is returned.

constraints: haystack and needle consist of only lowercase English characters
hints: Java's own String.indexOf() (from this lesson's Section 4) already solves this directly -- but writing it manually is good practice for understanding what indexOf does internally.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: At every possible starting position in haystack, check if needle matches character by character.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Java's built-in String.indexOf() is already highly optimized -- using it directly is the practical, real-world solution.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m) worst case internally, but highly optimized in practice

sections.title: Length of Last Word
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s consisting of words and spaces, return the length of the last word in the string. A word is a maximal substring consisting of non-space characters only.

examples.explanation: The last word is "World" with length 5.

examples.input: s = "   fly me   to   the moon  "

constraints: s consists of English letters and spaces
hints: Scan from the END of the string: skip trailing spaces first, then count characters until the next space (or the start of the string).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use String methods to trim and split the string, then take the last element's length.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Scan backward from the end: skip trailing spaces, then count non-space characters, without splitting or trimming.

sections.title: Reverse Words in a String
sections.problemStatement: Given an input string s, reverse the order of the words. A word is a maximal sequence of non-space characters. Return a string with words in reverse order, joined by a single space, with no leading, trailing, or extra spaces.
constraints: s contains English letters, digits, spaces, and can contain leading/trailing/multiple spaces
hints: split() with a regex handling multiple spaces, then rebuild in reverse order using StringBuilder.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Split on whitespace (handling multiple spaces), then manually build the reversed result with string concatenation.
approaches.output: For s = "the sky is blue": "blue is sky the"
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n^2) worst case, due to repeated String concatenation (recall Strings and Immutability)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Split on whitespace, then build the reversed result using StringBuilder instead of String concatenation, avoiding repeated object creation.
approaches.output: For s = "the sky is blue": "blue is sky the"
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the words array and StringBuilder

sections.title: String Compression
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of characters chars, compress it in place: each group of consecutive repeating characters is replaced by the character followed by its count (if more than 1). Return the new length of the array after compression.
constraints: chars[i] is a lowercase/uppercase English letter, digit, or symbol
hints: Two pointers: one (read) scans through counting each run of repeated characters, another (write) overwrites the array in place with the compressed form.
A count greater than 1 needs each of its digits written individually, since chars holds single characters, not Strings.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build the compressed result in a StringBuilder first, then copy it back into chars.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers (read/write) directly on the array -- no extra StringBuilder needed.
